sections.heading: Symptoms
sections.content: Testicular cancer most often presents as a painless or painful testicular nodule, mass, enlargement, or induration (hardening). Many patients present with testicular discomfort or swelling suggestive of epididymitis or orchitis. Germ cell tumors (GCTs) comprise 95% of malignant tumors arising in the testes and are broadly categorized into two main histologic subtypes: seminoma and nonseminoma. Seminomas and nonseminomas typically occur at about the same rate, but nonseminomas tend to be more aggressive and often include multiple cell types. The four types of nonseminomas are embryonal carcinoma, choriocarcinoma, yolk sac tumor, and teratoma. Most nonseminomas are mixed tumors of these four subtypes. Testicular cancer accounts for <1% of new cancer cases with a majority of patients being diagnosed between the ages of 20 and 34 years. The global incidence has been steadily rising over the past several decades, with an estimated 9,810 new cases projected in the United States in 2026 [MS-2]. Several risk factors have been identified, including personal or family history of testicular cancer and cryptorchidism [MS-2]. An estimated 630 deaths will occur from testicular cancer in the United States in 2026, reflecting the excellent 5-year relative survival rate (~94%) [MS-2].

symptoms.name: Painless testicular nodule or mass
symptoms.detail: Most common presentation; a firm, hard mass within the testis that may be painless or painful.

symptoms.name: Testicular discomfort or swelling
symptoms.detail: Often mistaken for or suggestive of epididymitis or orchitis; patients may have received a trial of antibiotics which is never warranted in a suspicious mass but can be considered in pain without a mass.

symptoms.name: Testicular induration (hardening)
symptoms.detail: Hardening of the testicular tissue detected on physical examination.

symptoms.name: Testicular enlargement
symptoms.detail: Diffuse or focal enlargement of the testis.

symptoms.name: Gynecomastia
symptoms.detail: Breast enlargement may occur due to hormonal effects of hCG-producing tumors.

symptoms.name: Low back or flank pain
symptoms.detail: May indicate retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy from metastatic disease.
symptoms.frequency: Less common; more typical of advanced disease

symptoms.name: Supraclavicular lymphadenopathy
symptoms.detail: Enlarged lymph nodes of the lower neck or upper chest, indicating lymphatic spread.

symptoms.name: Retroperitoneal mass symptoms
symptoms.detail: Abdominal fullness, nausea, or early satiety from large retroperitoneal nodal masses.

symptoms.name: Neurologic symptoms
symptoms.detail: Headache, seizures, focal deficits, or altered mental status may indicate brain metastases.

symptoms.name: Symptoms of disseminated disease
symptoms.detail: Weight loss, fatigue, dyspnea from pulmonary metastases, bone pain from skeletal metastases.

alarms.name: Red Flag
alarms.detail: May indicate retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy from metastatic disease.

alarms.name: Red Flag
alarms.detail: Enlarged lymph nodes of the lower neck or upper chest, indicating lymphatic spread.

alarms.name: Red Flag
alarms.detail: Abdominal fullness, nausea, or early satiety from large retroperitoneal nodal masses.

alarms.name: Red Flag
alarms.detail: Headache, seizures, focal deficits, or altered mental status may indicate brain metastases.

alarms.name: Red Flag
alarms.detail: Weight loss, fatigue, dyspnea from pulmonary metastases, bone pain from skeletal metastases.
